{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"**Triage Order**

1. `src/plugin.ts` — refactor first, but very carefully.
   It has the strongest blast-radius signal among files with an actionable function-level cut: `fan-in 10, fan-out 3`, with `fan-in: 10 importers (p95 cutoff: 9, p99: 10)`. It also has `buildFinished` at 103 lines. Because many files import it, I would avoid API reshaping at first and extract private helpers behind the same exports.

2. `src/utils.ts` — split selectively, not as a broad cleanup.
   This is the biggest shared utility hotspot: `fan-in 18, fan-out 1`, with `fan-in: 18 importers (p95 cutoff: 9, p99: 10)`. It is also a large file: `424 non-empty lines`, `33 top-level functions`. I would first split stable, low-level helpers into cohesive modules while preserving re-exports, because changing call sites all at once would create unnecessary risk.

3. `src/index.ts` — refactor after `plugin.ts` / `utils.ts`, focused on orchestration seams.
   It has both high connectivity and the largest local complexity: `fan-in 4, fan-out 17`, with `fan-out: 17 imports (p95 cutoff: 6, p99: 11)`. It is also a large file at `459 non-empty lines`, with `28 top-level functions`, and has the biggest functions: `build` is 293 lines, anonymous wrapper 274 lines, `mainTasks` 194 lines. I would extract internal build phases first, keeping the public entrypoint stable.

4. `src/esbuild/index.ts` — refactor after `src/index.ts`, or in parallel only if ownership is clear.
   It has high outward dependency coupling: `fan-in 1, fan-out 11`, with `fan-out: 11 imports (p95 cutoff: 6, p99: 11)`. It also has `runEsbuild` at 237 lines. This is a good candidate for extracting option normalization, plugin assembly, and execution/reporting helpers, but the fan-out means every extraction should be backed by tests around generated config/output behavior.

5. `src/esbuild/postcss.ts` / `src/esbuild/svelte.ts` — medium priority plugin-local reductions.
   These are not listed as high fan-in/fan-out, but they have large plugin setup bodies. `postcssPlugin` is 140 lines and `setup` is 126 lines; `sveltePlugin` is 116 lines and `setup` is 106 lines. I would refactor these after the central hotspots because their blast radius appears lower and the risk is mostly local readability/testability.

6. `src/api-extractor.ts`, `src/tsc.ts`, `src/rollup.ts` — targeted cleanup, not first-wave blast-radius work.
   `src/api-extractor.ts` has `fan-in 1, fan-out 6`, exactly at the p95 fan-out cutoff, and an 85-line `rollupDtsFiles`. `src/rollup.ts` has `fan-in 0, fan-out 7`, but `blast_radius` is 0 and no importers are reported. `src/tsc.ts` has a 73-line function but no high connectivity finding. I would mainly address duplicated `getDuration` / clock handling across these files rather than large structural refactors.

**Leave Alone For Now**

`src/log.ts` has `fan-in 9, fan-out 0`, so it is load-bearing, but there is no large-file or large-function finding attached. I would avoid refactoring it unless there is a concrete logging change.

`test/utils.ts` also has `fan-in 9, fan-out 0`, but it is test infrastructure with low agent risk and no large-function/large-file finding. Leave it alone unless test helper behavior is actively blocking safer coverage.

`src/rollup/ts-resolve.ts`, `src/plugins/swc-target.ts`, `src/plugins/terser.ts`, and `src/esbuild/swc.ts` have medium large-function findings, but either low blast radius or only slightly exceed thresholds. They are cleanup candidates after the central files, not blast-radius reducers."}}
